checks.a_analysis: The output document does not contain any fabricated paragraphs or sentences. All text content, excluding AI-generated image descriptions and table metadata (which are permitted), is derived from the source document.
No contact information (phone numbers, emails, URLs) is present in either the source or the output.
All primary sections (heading, map, legend) are present in the output. While some footer metadata is missing, it is not considered a complete section in the context of this document.

The legend table in the source contains two columns of lists. The output represents this as a single-row table with two columns, where each cell contains the comma-separated list of items from the corresponding column. No data items are missing from the table.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output omits the metadata found at the bottom of the source document, including the map scale and spatial reference information. Specifically, 'Scale: 1:3,179,372', '1 inch equals 50 miles', 'Spatial Reference Name: NAD 1983 StatePlane North Carolina FIPS 3200 Feet', and 'Status as of: 6/16/2025' (bottom right instance) are missing. This missing text accounts for approximately 40% of the total textual information on the page, exceeding the 25% threshold for a major defect.
checks.b_evidence_source: Scale: 1:3,179,372, 1 inch equals 50 miles, Spatial Reference Name: NAD 1983 StatePlane North Carolina FIPS 3200 Feet, Status as of: 6/16/2025
checks.b_evidence_output: [Information completely missing from JSON text and alt text]
checks.b_evidence_location: Bottom footer area of the page

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: Other than the omissions noted in C5, the extracted text faithfully represents the source content. Minor reformatting in the table (using commas instead of a vertical list) is acceptable.

checks.a_analysis: The output document correctly represents the content of the source PDF without any duplication of text blocks. The title 'Building Footprint Update Status' appears once in the metadata and once as the primary heading for the section, which is expected behavior.

The output contains a single top-level heading (the section title 'Building Footprint Update Status'). There are no subheadings, so the hierarchy is flat but not broken. This is appropriate for a single-page document with one main title.

The document's legend is represented as a table in the JSON. While the source legend is a visual block of keys and descriptions, converting it into a structured table with appropriate headers ('Phases' and 'Status Indicators') is a logical and acceptable structural transformation for accessibility.

The reading order in the JSON follows the logical top-to-bottom and left-to-right flow of the source document: Header Logo -> Title -> Map -> Legend -> Footer Logos. This maintains the structural fidelity of the original layout.
The legend content, which contains list-like data, has been correctly grouped within the table structure. No other lists exist in the source document.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The metadata indicates 0 headings in the JSON and 1 in the HTML. The discrepancy of 1 is well within the allowed tolerance of 3 headings. This typically occurs when the section title is rendered as an H1 in the HTML but not counted as a separate heading element in the JSON metadata.

There are no links in the source document. The HTML metadata shows 1 link in the HTML vs 0 in the JSON; this is likely an automatically injected 'skip link' or metadata link by the renderer, which is an acceptable accessibility feature.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization of the source PDF. The title and the primary content (the map) are prominently displayed. While the layout of logos and the addition of a 'Map Legend Summary' table differ from the PDF's single-page graphic layout, these changes are consistent with responsive HTML design and accessibility improvements. The flow of information is logical and mirrors the source's intent.

The HTML output introduces a 'Map Legend Summary' table that is not explicitly a separate table in the PDF (where it is part of the map image). This table is well-rendered with distinct headers and clearly categorized data, making the map's legend accessible as text.

All meaningful images from the source PDF, including the DPS banner, the North Carolina Emergency Management triangle logo, the EMAP Accredited logo, and the main Risk Building Footprint Refresh map, are present and correctly rendered in the HTML output.
There is no evidence of visual content duplication. Each section, logo, and text element appears only once in the HTML output.

The overall visual quality of the HTML document is high. The text is legible, images are clear, and the layout is professional. The addition of the legend summary table enhances the document's utility and accessibility without compromising its appearance.
